macro_segments.title: Defining the Oref: Location of Melika vs. Shechita and the Meaning of 'Mimul Orpo'

micro_segments.title: Introduction: Shechita from the Throat vs. Melika from the Back of the Neck
Gemara's Question: What Does 'Oref' Mean — Back of Head or Back of Neck?
Proof from 'Kol Ha-Oref Kasher Le-Melika' That Oref Means the Neck
Baraita: Mimul Orpo — Facing the Oref, Derived from 've-Hu Yosheiv Mimuli'
Second Verse: 'Ki Fanu Eilai Oref' Establishes That Oref Is Opposite the Face
Digression: Am Keshei Oref — Stiff-Necked People and the Meaning of Oref

macro_segments.title: Bnei Rabbi Chiya: Moving the Simanim Behind the Oref for Melika — Debate on Whether This Is Required or Optional

micro_segments.title: Bnei Rabbi Chiya: The Mitzva of Melika Requires Moving the Simanim to Behind the Oref
Two Versions: Is Moving the Simanim Required (Davka) or Just Acceptable (Af Machzir)?
Proof That Af Machzir Is Correct: If Machzir Were Davka, Shechita from the Back Would Also Be Valid
Rav Yannai Rejects Bnei Rabbi Chiya: The Mishnah's Summary Excludes Machzir Simanim from Melika
Rabbah Bar Bar Chana: The Mishnah's Summary Refers to Shen ve-Tzipporen, Not Machzir Simanim
Tosafot's Reading: Could a Kohen Perform Melika With His Tooth?
Rabbi Yirmiya: The Mishnah Excludes Molich u-Mevi — Distinguishing Cutting from Piercing Motion
Resolution: Bnei Rabbi Chiya Hold That Molich u-Mevi Is Invalid for Melika
Summary of Differences Between Melika and Shechita: Location, Motion, and Instrument

macro_segments.title: Rav Kahana on Kotzetz ve-Yored and the Debate Over Molich u-Mevi in Melika

micro_segments.title: Rav Kahana: The Mitzva of Melika Is Performed by Cutting Downward (Kotzetz ve-Yored)
Rav Ami Assumes Molich u-Mevi Is Invalid; Rabbi Yirmiya Argues It Is Certainly Valid
Shmuel's Rule: Whatever Is Valid for Shechita Is Valid for Melika from the Back
Implication: What Is Invalid for Shechita Is Invalid for Melika — What Case Does This Exclude?
Attempt to Exclude Ikur Simanim, Rejected: Ein Ikur Simanim ba-Of (Rami Bar Yechezkel)
Rav Pappa: The Rule Excludes Shefui Rosh — Starting Too High, the Problem of Hagrama in Melika

macro_segments.title: Ikur Simanim in Birds: Biblical vs. Rabbinic Shechita and Whether Ikur Applies to Melika

micro_segments.title: Rav Acha: Ein Ikur Simanim ba-Of Applies Only if Shechita of Birds Is Rabbinic
Counterargument: If Shechita of Birds Is Biblical, There Is More Room for Unique Bird Laws Like Ein Ikur
If Shechita of Birds Is Rabbinic, It Should Be Modeled Fully After Beheimah — Including Ikur
Ravina: Ein Ikur Simanim ba-Of Is Limited to Melika, Not to Ordinary Shechita of Birds
This View Disagrees with Shmuel's Principle That What Is Valid for Shechita Is Valid for Melika

macro_segments.title: Zeiri's Ruling on Nishbera Mafreket: Is Breaking the Neck a Neveila or a Treifa — Relevance to Brain Death

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The Relationship Between Shechita, Death, and Halachic Categories
Example of Ben Noach and Eiver Min ha-Chai After Shechita: Is the Animal Halachically Dead?
Zeiri: Breaking the Spine and Most of the Flesh Makes the Animal a Neveila
Rav Chisda's Support: Melika with a Knife Is a Neveila, Not a Treifa
Rejection: The Neveila Status from Melika with a Knife Is Due to Invalid Shechita Act (Dores or Machlid), Not Prior Death
Rava's Question: How Can Melika Be Performed on an Animal That Is Already Halachically Dead?
Abbaye's Parallel: Cutting the Second Siman of an Olah Bird After the First — Also on a 'Dead' Bird?
Rava's Answer: Cutting the Second Siman Is for Havdalah (Severing the Head), Not for Shechita
Rava's Resolution: Melika Must Be Done Without Severing Most of the Flesh — Keeping the Bird Halachically Alive Until the Simanim Are Cut
Rashi's Clarification: Every Shechita Creates a Treifa Midway — That Is Not a Problem; Only Prior Neveila Invalidates
Relevance to Brain Death: Is Neveila the Same as Meisa? Rambam's Language and the Halachic Definition of Death

topical_tags.term: Ben Noach and eiver min ha-chai
